---
title: "Practical Skills: Git, GitHub & AI"
subtitle: "AEM 7010: Doing Applied Economics Research<br>Prof. Ariel Ortiz-Bobea"
---

## About this site

This site covers **sessions 4–8** of AEM 7010. Sessions 1–3 (introduction and reproducibility with Lars Vilhuber) are taught separately and are not included here.

## What you will learn

| Module | Sessions | Topics |
|--------|----------|--------|
| **Version Control** | 4–5 | Git fundamentals, GitHub, branches, pull requests, tags, collaboration |
| **AI Tools**        | 6–8 | Chat, agentic desktop (Cowork), code-native agents (Claude Code), verification |

By the end of these five sessions you will be able to:

- Track changes to your R scripts and research code using Git
- Back up your work on GitHub and collaborate with co-authors through pull requests
- Tag specific versions of your code for paper submissions and replication packages
- Use AI coding assistants responsibly in research
- Document and disclose AI use in a way that meets journal and replication standards

## Sessions

**Session 4: Git Fundamentals.** The core Git workflow on your own computer: `init`, `add`, `commit`, `log`, `diff`, `.gitignore`, and undoing mistakes.

**Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration.** Connect to GitHub, push and pull, create branches, open pull requests, and tag releases for reproducibility.

**Session 6: AI Tools I (Chat).** A mental model of LLMs. Mode A vs Mode B discipline. Live demo of a chat-driven scrape of Cornell Dyson placements, then a hands-on exercise where each student scrapes a different department.

**Session 7: AI Tools II (Cowork).** Agentic desktop AI that can see your files and run code. Guided scrape of the Dyson page with Cowork, stress test on a modified field, verification checklist.

**Session 8: AI Tools III (Claude Code).** A code-native agent inside a git project. Scaffold a five-department scraper, add a rule-based plus LLM-based classifier, produce a descriptive write-up of PhD placements in applied economics.

## Before You Begin

Please complete the following before **Session 4**.

1. **Install Git** on your laptop
    - Mac: `brew install git` (if you have Homebrew) or download from [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com)
    - Windows: download the installer from [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com)
2. **Open a terminal**
    - Mac: open the Terminal app (search for "Terminal" in Spotlight)
    - Windows: open Git Bash (installed with Git)
3. **Verify the installation** by running:

```bash
git --version
```

You should see something like `git version 2.39.0`.

4. **Install R and an editor or IDE.** Sessions 4–5 show three ways to use Git: Terminal, RStudio, and VS Code. You will follow the Terminal in class, but having an editor also set up lets you try the GUI alternatives at home. Sessions 6–7 assume you have both editors available for the AI-coding demos.

    - **R** is required for every code example in this site and for the `wooldridge` package. Download the installer for your platform from [cran.r-project.org](https://cran.r-project.org). Free and open source.
    - **RStudio** is the standard IDE for applied economics research in R. If you already use it for coursework, you are set. Otherwise, download from [posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/).
    - **VS Code** is a general-purpose code editor that has become the most widely used tool in software and data work. Download from [code.visualstudio.com](https://code.visualstudio.com).

    I encourage you to install all three. R is the engine. RStudio is ideal for R-centric work. VS Code is ideal for everything else: Python, shell scripts, LaTeX, Quarto, Markdown, editing configuration files, and working with AI coding assistants.

5. **Install the `wooldridge` R package** so the tutorial's data examples work without any downloads. In R or RStudio, run:

    ```r
    install.packages("wooldridge")
    ```

    This gives you the classic datasets from Jeffrey Wooldridge's *Introductory Econometrics* textbook (`wage1`, `wage2`, and many others) that we use throughout the exercises.

Before **Session 5**, also **create a free GitHub account** at [github.com](https://github.com).

::: {.callout-note}
## What is Homebrew?
Homebrew is the standard macOS package manager. Think of it as an app store for command-line tools. If you do not already have it, run the one-line install command from [brew.sh](https://brew.sh). If you prefer to skip Homebrew, use the direct download from [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com) instead.
:::

## Companion Materials

- [Session 4 slides](version-control/slides/session4.qmd) (web-based, click through in your browser)
- [Session 5 slides](version-control/session5_github_collaboration.pptx) (PowerPoint, being migrated)
- [Exercise R scripts](version-control/exercises/)
